title: Splitting the Flow: Baseflow Separation
description: Run the Lyne–Hollick digital filter interactively — drag the α slider to rewrite the live Python cell in real time, compare filtered vs raw hydrographs side by side, and understand why the Baseflow Index matters for drought assessment and groundwater recharge estimation.

citation.text: Lyne, V. & Hollick, M. (1979). Stochastic time-variable rainfall-runoff modelling. Inst. Eng. Australia Nat. Conf. Publ. 79/10, 89–93.

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: Capstone: A Basin's Water Story
description: Synthesis capstone for the Intro to Hydrology course. Choose one of four curated basins (or any USGS gauge), fetch a 10-year streamflow record, compute hydrological signatures (BFI, FDC), run a TWI terrain analysis, then narrate the basin's water story through a 6-stage interactive timeline. Generates a structured text report you can export.

citation.text: Addor, N. et al. (2017). The CAMELS data set: catchment attributes and meteorology for large-sample studies. Hydrol. Earth Syst. Sci., 21, 5293–5313.

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: Congratulations: Course Complete
description: Collect your name, preview a branded certificate of completion, and print or save it as a permanent record of finishing the Introduction to Hydrology course.

citation.text: AI-Hydro Learning Hub — Introduction to Hydrology (2026).

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: The Shape of the Land: DEM to TWI
description: Compute the Topographic Wetness Index from a DEM step by step — click cells to trace D8 flow paths, explore the result on a 3-D draggable terrain, and compare elevation vs wetness surfaces side by side with a wipe slider. Includes D8 vs D∞ comparison and a detailed explainer on why ln() matters.

citation.text: Beven, K.J. & Kirkby, M.J. (1979). A physically based, variable contributing area model of basin hydrology. Hydrol. Sci. Bull., 24(1), 43–69.

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: Reading a Hydrograph
description: Anatomy of a storm hydrograph — rising limb, peak, recession — with an interactive BFI explorer, a storm-pair comparison revealing non-linear runoff response, and a live Python cell that generates CAMELS-style annual hydrographs for three contrasting gauges.

citation.text: Lyne, V. & Hollick, M. (1979). Stochastic time-variable rainfall-runoff modelling. Inst. Eng. Australia Nat. Conf. Publ. 79/10, 89–93.

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: Catching the Flow: Real Streamflow Data
description: Pull live USGS records for four curated CONUS gauges spanning flood-prone Maine to arid Arizona — or enter any gauge ID. Plot the annual regime, spot a flood event, and read a flow-duration curve. Falls back to seeded synthetic data when the network is unavailable.

citation.text: USGS National Water Information System (NWIS). https://waterdata.usgs.gov/

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: The Water Cycle in Motion
description: Trace a water molecule through all six fluxes — evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, infiltration, runoff — via a Manim animation and a draggable SVG timeline. Run the Python cell to watch the cycle come to life, then use the mass-balance simulator to see why precipitation must equal ET + R + ΔS.

citation.text: Trenberth, K.E. et al. (2007). Estimates of the global water budget. J. Hydrometeorol., 8(4), 758–769.

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University

title: The Watershed: Where Rain Becomes Runoff
description: Define a catchment, trace D8 flow paths across a 3-D draggable terrain, and drop a virtual rain cloud over the basin to watch the hydrograph respond. Covers watershed boundaries, flow accumulation, and the link between contributing area and streamflow.

citation.text: O'Callaghan, J.F. & Mark, D.M. (1984). The extraction of drainage networks from digital elevation data. Comput. Vision Graph. Image Process., 28, 323–344.

author: Mohammad Galib, Purdue University
